quiz.question: She found it difficult to conform to the social norms of her new workplace. What does 'conform' mean?
options: To comply with rules or standards — to behave in the expected way
To adapt her personal style to what colleagues expected
To accept constraints that she found uncomfortable
To change her behavior to avoid standing out as different, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To comply with rules or standards — to behave in the expected way
quiz.explanation:
To conform means to comply — to act in accordance with expected norms or rules.
• conform = to comply with rules or standards; to be similar
• From Latin: 'conformare' — to form after
• 'Conform to expectations', 'non-conformist' — following or breaking norms

quiz.question: It is a social norm in many cultures to greet strangers with a handshake. What is a norm?
options: A standard or pattern of behaviour that is expected or typical
A rule of etiquette that applies in formal situations
A custom so widely practised it has the force of a social rule, as the evidence showed
A behaviour that marks the practitioner as educated and polite
quiz.answer: A standard or pattern of behaviour that is expected or typical
quiz.explanation:
A norm is a standard of behaviour — what is considered typical or expected.
• norm = a standard; typical behaviour; what is considered normal
• From Latin: 'norma' — a carpenter's square
• 'Social norm', 'the norm', 'abnormal' — the standard or rule

quiz.question: He faced social ostracism after speaking out against the community's most powerful figure. What is ostracism?
options: Exclusion from a group or community by common consent
A consequence imposed informally by those who had been offended
A social punishment reserved for those who broke unwritten rules
A coordinated withdrawal of acceptance from a community member
quiz.answer: Exclusion from a group or community by common consent
quiz.explanation:
Ostracism is exclusion — deliberate social exclusion by collective decision.
• ostracism = exclusion from society or a group
• From Greek: 'ostrakismos' — banishment by vote on pottery shards
• 'Social ostracism', 'face ostracism' — being shut out collectively

quiz.question: Medical empiricism demands that treatments be tested and proved before they are adopted. What is empiricism?
options: The view that knowledge comes from observation and experiment
The principle that science must build on what can be tested
A philosophical position that rejects speculation in favour of evidence
The framework underpinning the scientific method
quiz.answer: The view that knowledge comes from observation and experiment
quiz.explanation:
Empiricism is the philosophical position that knowledge derives from experience.
• empiricism = the theory that all knowledge is based on sensory experience
• From Greek: 'empeiria' — experience
• 'Scientific empiricism', 'empirical method' — knowledge from observation

quiz.question: Her literary eclecticism meant she drew on sources ranging from philosophy to pulp fiction. What is eclecticism?
options: The practice of selecting ideas from a wide variety of sources
An approach that valued breadth of reading over depth in any one area
A way of working that combined influences others kept separate
A creative philosophy that resisted being defined by a single tradition
quiz.answer: The practice of selecting ideas from a wide variety of sources
quiz.explanation:
Eclecticism is the practice of drawing from diverse sources — not committed to one system.
• eclecticism = selecting from various sources or traditions
• From Greek: 'eklektikos' — selective
• 'Eclectic taste', 'eclectic approach' — drawing from many different sources

quiz.question: The speech was full of witticisms that kept the audience laughing. What is a witticism?
options: A witty remark — a clever and amusing observation
A joke polished enough to be remembered after the event
A piece of wordplay that demonstrated real verbal skill
An observation so sharp it earned applause as well as laughter
quiz.answer: A witty remark — a clever and amusing observation
quiz.explanation:
A witticism is a clever, witty remark — a polished amusing observation.
• witticism = a witty or clever remark
• From 'witty' + '-icism'
• 'A well-turned witticism', 'exchange witticisms' — clever, humorous remarks

quiz.question: Chronic absenteeism is defined as missing more than ten percent of school days. What is absenteeism?
options: The practice or habit of being regularly absent without good reason
A pattern of non-attendance that affects academic performance, as the evidence showed
A behaviour requiring intervention from school and family
A problem measured by reference to an attendance threshold
quiz.answer: The practice or habit of being regularly absent without good reason
quiz.explanation:
Absenteeism is habitual absence — the practice of repeatedly failing to attend.
• absenteeism = habitual absence from work or school
• From 'absent' + '-ism'
• 'Worker absenteeism', 'chronic absenteeism' — persistent non-attendance

quiz.question: The debate between theism and atheism has occupied philosophers for centuries. What is atheism?
options: The belief that no god or gods exist
The philosophical position that rejects all religious claims
A worldview that explains existence without reference to divinity
The rejection of theistic accounts of the universe
quiz.answer: The belief that no god or gods exist
quiz.explanation:
Atheism is the rejection of belief in gods — the position that no deity exists.
• atheism = disbelief in the existence of god or gods
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'theos' (god)
• 'Atheist', 'atheistic argument' — the position that there is no god

quiz.question: Words like 'selfie' and 'tweet' are neologisms that entered English in the digital age. What is a neologism?
options: A newly coined word or expression
A word invented to describe a phenomenon that did not previously exist
A term that entered common use quickly because of the internet
A word whose meaning is still being established in common usage
quiz.answer: A newly coined word or expression
quiz.explanation:
A neologism is a new word — recently coined or coming into use.
• neologism = a newly coined word or phrase
• From Greek: 'neo-' (new) + 'logos' (word)
• 'Tech neologism', 'slang neologism' — new words entering the language

quiz.question: A schism over economic policy split the party into two irreconcilable factions. What is a schism?
options: A formal division within a group — a split over a fundamental disagreement
A dispute that revealed differences which had been present for years, as the evidence showed
A fracture caused by two opposing views on a critical question
A division that reduced the effectiveness of the organisation
quiz.answer: A formal division within a group — a split over a fundamental disagreement
quiz.explanation:
A schism is a division — a formal split caused by deep disagreement.
• schism = a split or division, especially in a church or organisation
• From Greek: 'skhisma' — a division
• 'Church schism', 'political schism' — a deep organisational split

quiz.question: The debate was full of sophisms designed to mislead rather than illuminate. What is a sophism?
options: A clever but deliberately misleading argument
A logical-sounding claim whose purpose was to confuse
An argument that concealed a false premise behind technical language
A rhetorical device that produced agreement without justification
quiz.answer: A clever but deliberately misleading argument
quiz.explanation:
A sophism is a specious argument — clever in appearance but fundamentally misleading.
• sophism = a plausible but fallacious argument
• From Greek: 'sophisma' — a clever device
• 'Sophist', 'sophistical argument' — clever but deceptive reasoning

quiz.question: Surrealism sought to express the unconscious mind through dreamlike imagery. What is surrealism?
options: An art movement that expressed the unconscious through dream-like imagery
A style developed in Paris in the 1920s by artists influenced by Freud
An approach that rejected rational thought in favour of the irrational
A movement that combined unexpected elements to produce psychological impact
quiz.answer: An art movement that expressed the unconscious through dream-like imagery
quiz.explanation:
Surrealism is an art movement exploring the unconscious — dreamlike and irrational.
• surrealism = an art movement using dreamlike imagery to explore the unconscious
• From French: 'sur-' (above) + 'réalisme' (realism)
• Dalí, Magritte, Ernst — surrealists; 'surreal' = dreamlike, bizarre

quiz.question: The speech used parallelism to give its key phrases a memorable rhythm. What is parallelism?
options: The use of matching grammatical structures to create balance and rhythm
A rhetorical device that repeated words at the start of successive clauses
A technique that made arguments easier to follow through repetition of form
A stylistic choice that gave equal weight to equally important points
quiz.answer: The use of matching grammatical structures to create balance and rhythm
quiz.explanation:
Parallelism is balanced grammatical structure — repeating forms for rhetorical effect.
• parallelism = the use of matching grammatical forms
• From Greek: 'parallelos' — beside one another
• 'I came, I saw, I conquered' — parallel structure; used for emphasis and rhythm

quiz.question: The sport of pugilism requires not only strength but also strategy and footwork. What is pugilism?
options: The art or practice of boxing — fighting with the fists
A combat sport governed by rules and administered by referees
A physical discipline requiring conditioning, technique, and courage
A sport in which competitors are matched by weight class
quiz.answer: The art or practice of boxing — fighting with the fists
quiz.explanation:
Pugilism is boxing — the sport or practice of fighting with the fists.
• pugilism = the sport of boxing
• From Latin: 'pugil' — a boxer
• 'Pugilist', 'pugilistic career' — boxer and boxing

quiz.question: Nihilism holds that life has no meaning, value, or purpose. What is nihilism?
options: The belief that life is without meaning, purpose, or value
A philosophical position that denies the possibility of objective truth
A rejection of all moral and religious principles
The view that nothing in existence has inherent significance
quiz.answer: The belief that life is without meaning, purpose, or value
quiz.explanation:
Nihilism is the rejection of meaning — the view that life has no purpose or value.
• nihilism = the rejection of all moral and religious principles; the belief in nothing
• From Latin: 'nihil' — nothing
• 'Nietzsche on nihilism', 'nihilist outlook' — denying meaning

quiz.question: She used the euphemism 'passed away' to soften the fact of her father's death. What is a euphemism?
options: A mild expression used in place of a harsher or more direct one
A form of words chosen to reduce the emotional impact of a statement
A way of referring to something unpleasant without naming it directly
A substitute for language that would cause offence or distress
quiz.answer: A mild expression used in place of a harsher or more direct one
quiz.explanation:
A euphemism is a gentle substitute — a less direct way of saying something uncomfortable.
• euphemism = a mild or polite expression used instead of a harsh one
• From Greek: 'eu-' (well) + 'pheme' (speaking)
• 'Passed away' for died; 'let go' for fired — euphemisms soften reality

quiz.question: His deep pessimism about the future made him reluctant to invest in anything. What is pessimism?
options: A tendency to expect the worst — a negative view of the future
A philosophical outlook in which positive outcomes were always doubted
A disposition shaped by repeated disappointments
An attitude that made planning difficult because it assumed failure
quiz.answer: A tendency to expect the worst — a negative view of the future
quiz.explanation:
Pessimism is the tendency to expect bad outcomes — a negative outlook.
• pessimism = expecting the worst; a gloomy outlook
• From Latin: 'pessimus' — the worst
• Opposite of optimism; 'pessimist' — one who always expects the worst

quiz.question: The doctors expressed guarded optimism about the patient's recovery. What is optimism?
options: A tendency to expect favourable outcomes — a positive outlook
A disposition that interpreted available evidence positively
A professional stance that communicated hope without making promises
A reading of the situation that emphasised what was going well
quiz.answer: A tendency to expect favourable outcomes — a positive outlook
quiz.explanation:
Optimism is the tendency to expect good outcomes — a positive view of the future.
• optimism = expecting the best; a positive outlook
• From Latin: 'optimus' — the best
• Opposite of pessimism; 'guarded optimism' = cautiously positive

quiz.question: Every living organism, from bacteria to blue whales, shares certain fundamental characteristics. What is an organism?
options: A living individual — any form of life functioning as a whole
A biological entity capable of growth, reproduction, and response
Any living system composed of cells and following genetic instructions
A life form occupying a specific ecological niche
quiz.answer: A living individual — any form of life functioning as a whole
quiz.explanation:
An organism is a living individual — any living thing functioning as a system.
• organism = a living individual; any life form
• From Greek: 'organon' — a tool
• 'Single-celled organism', 'complex organism' — any living entity

quiz.question: Microorganisms grow rapidly when provided with nutrients and warm conditions. What is a microorganism?
options: A living organism too small to be seen with the naked eye
A single-celled life form of the kind studied in microbiology
A type of organism associated with both disease and fermentation
A biological entity whose existence was unknown before the microscope
quiz.answer: A living organism too small to be seen with the naked eye
quiz.explanation:
A microorganism is a microscopic life form — too small to be seen unaided.
• microorganism = a living organism visible only under a microscope
• From Greek: 'mikros' (small) + 'organon' (tool)
• Bacteria, viruses, and fungi — all microorganisms

quiz.question: Denial is a common defence mechanism used to avoid confronting unpleasant truths. What is a mechanism?
options: A system or process by which something is carried out
A psychological process operating to protect the conscious mind
A way of functioning that achieves a specific psychological outcome
A response pattern activated in situations of psychological stress
quiz.answer: A system or process by which something is carried out
quiz.explanation:
A mechanism is a system by which an outcome is achieved — a process or method.
• mechanism = a system by which something operates
• From Greek: 'mechane' — a machine
• 'Defence mechanism', 'pricing mechanism' — a system or process

quiz.question: Cosmopolitanism holds that all people belong to a single world community. What is cosmopolitanism?
options: The view that all human beings are citizens of a single world
A philosophy that placed global solidarity above national loyalty
An ideal in which cultural differences were valued and preserved
A political vision of world government and international law
quiz.answer: The view that all human beings are citizens of a single world
quiz.explanation:
Cosmopolitanism is the view that all are world citizens — belonging to one global community.
• cosmopolitanism = the idea that all people belong to a single world community
• From Greek: 'kosmos' (world) + 'polites' (citizen)
• 'Cosmopolitan city', 'cosmopolitan ideals' — belonging to the whole world

quiz.question: His rabid chauvinism made him incapable of acknowledging the achievements of other nations. What is chauvinism?
options: Excessive and aggressive belief in the superiority of one's own group
A nationalism so strong it produced contempt for other countries
A form of bias that dismissed any evidence against one's own side, as the evidence showed
A prejudice that operated as both a belief and an identity
quiz.answer: Excessive and aggressive belief in the superiority of one's own group
quiz.explanation:
Chauvinism is aggressive conviction of superiority — excessive and unreasoning.
• chauvinism = exaggerated loyalty or belief in the superiority of one's group
• Named after Nicolas Chauvin — a notoriously devoted soldier
• 'Male chauvinism', 'national chauvinism' — excessive group loyalty and bias

quiz.question: His philosophy was one of frank hedonism — pleasure above all else. What is hedonism?
options: The pursuit of pleasure as the primary goal of life
A philosophy that placed personal enjoyment above moral constraints
A way of living that rejected the idea of duty or self-denial
A set of values in which comfort and gratification were central
quiz.answer: The pursuit of pleasure as the primary goal of life
quiz.explanation:
Hedonism is the philosophy of pleasure — living to maximise enjoyment.
• hedonism = the pursuit of pleasure as the highest good
• From Greek: 'hedone' — pleasure
• 'Hedonist', 'hedonistic lifestyle' — devoted to pleasure

quiz.question: The open antagonism between the two departments made collaboration impossible. What is antagonism?
options: Active hostility — opposition between parties in conflict
A mutual dislike intense enough to disrupt shared work
A competitive dynamic that had curdled into outright hostility
A tension that was visible to anyone who attended a joint meeting
quiz.answer: Active hostility — opposition between parties in conflict
quiz.explanation:
Antagonism is active hostility — the state of being actively opposed.
• antagonism = active opposition; hostility
• From Greek: 'antagonizesthai' — to compete against
• 'Antagonist', 'mutual antagonism' — opposition and hostility

quiz.question: His egoism made genuine friendship difficult — every conversation returned to himself. What is egoism?
options: Excessive preoccupation with oneself — placing one's own interests above all
A character trait that prevented real engagement with others
A self-centredness that made him a poor listener and worse friend, as the evidence showed
A way of relating that used others primarily as an audience
quiz.answer: Excessive preoccupation with oneself — placing one's own interests above all
quiz.explanation:
Egoism is self-centredness — excessive concern with one's own interests and person.
• egoism = excessive self-interest; the view that self-interest is the chief motive
• From Latin: 'ego' — I
• 'Egoist', 'egotism' — preoccupation with oneself

quiz.question: The politician's jingoism appealed to voters who wanted strong national leadership. What is jingoism?
options: Extreme nationalism combined with aggressive foreign policy
A rhetorical style that framed international relations as competition
Patriotism expressed through hostility toward other nations
A form of nationalism that demanded military strength as a priority
quiz.answer: Extreme nationalism combined with aggressive foreign policy
quiz.explanation:
Jingoism is aggressive nationalism — bellicose national pride demanding confrontation.
• jingoism = extreme nationalism expressing itself in aggressive foreign policy
• From a British music-hall song of 1878 ('by jingo')
• 'Jingoist', 'jingoistic rhetoric' — belligerent nationalism

quiz.question: Reading fantasy novels was her favourite form of escapism. What is escapism?
options: The tendency to seek distraction from difficult realities
A preference for entertainment that required no serious engagement
A way of spending leisure time that offered relief from work
A habit that provided pleasure precisely because it required nothing
quiz.answer: The tendency to seek distraction from difficult realities
quiz.explanation:
Escapism is the tendency to seek relief from reality — through fantasy or entertainment.
• escapism = the tendency to seek distraction from unpleasant realities
• From 'escape' + '-ism'
• 'Escapist fiction', 'pure escapism' — entertainment as flight from reality

quiz.question: The character's malapropisms — saying 'dance a flamingo' instead of 'flamenco' — were a running joke. What is a malapropism?
options: The mistaken use of a word in place of a similar-sounding one
A comic error in which the wrong word was used in a phrase
A slip of language that exposed the speaker's limited vocabulary
A verbal mistake that produced an unintentionally funny effect
quiz.answer: The mistaken use of a word in place of a similar-sounding one
quiz.explanation:
A malapropism is a word error — substituting a wrong word for a similar-sounding one.
• malapropism = using the wrong word in place of a similar-sounding one
• Named after Mrs Malaprop in Sheridan's The Rivals
• 'Dance a flamingo' for 'flamenco' — a comic word substitution

quiz.question: The student faced a plagiarism investigation after submitting work copied from a published article. What is plagiarism?
options: Presenting another's work or ideas as one's own
The act of copying published material without acknowledgment
A form of academic dishonesty with serious institutional consequences
The unattributed use of another person's intellectual work
quiz.answer: Presenting another's work or ideas as one's own
quiz.explanation:
Plagiarism is intellectual theft — taking credit for another's work.
• plagiarism = the practice of taking another's work and presenting it as one's own
• From Latin: 'plagiarius' — a kidnapper
• 'Commit plagiarism', 'plagiarism detector' — academic or creative theft

quiz.question: Mesmerism was an early form of hypnosis that fascinated audiences in the 18th century. What is mesmerism?
options: An early technique of hypnotic induction — the precursor to hypnotism
A performance art that combined suggestion with theatrical spectacle
A pseudoscientific practice later incorporated into hypnotherapy
A method of inducing a trance state through eye contact and suggestion
quiz.answer: An early technique of hypnotic induction — the precursor to hypnotism
quiz.explanation:
Mesmerism is early hypnosis — the technique developed by Franz Mesmer.
• mesmerism = an early form of hypnotism
• Named after Franz Mesmer (1734–1815)
• 'Mesmerised' = hypnotised, or captivated; 'mesmeric power' — hypnotic

quiz.question: Her writing was full of pithy aphorisms that lingered long after the book was closed. What is an aphorism?
options: A short, memorable statement expressing a general truth
A phrase so well-crafted it was remembered and quoted by readers
A sentence that compressed significant insight into compact form
A saying whose meaning was clear without further explanation
quiz.answer: A short, memorable statement expressing a general truth
quiz.explanation:
An aphorism is a pithy truth — a short, memorable statement of a general principle.
• aphorism = a brief, memorable statement of a general truth
• From Greek: 'aphorismos' — a definition
• 'Hippocratic aphorisms', 'pithy aphorism' — compressed wisdom

quiz.question: His rigid dogmatism prevented him from considering evidence that challenged his views. What is dogmatism?
options: The tendency to assert opinions without accepting contrary evidence
A way of thinking that substituted authority for reason
An intellectual style that resisted revision in the face of new data
A disposition that treated established views as unquestionable
quiz.answer: The tendency to assert opinions without accepting contrary evidence
quiz.explanation:
Dogmatism is the assertion of beliefs as fact — closed to contrary evidence.
• dogmatism = laying down doctrines as if they were beyond question
• From Greek: 'dogma' — an established opinion
• 'Dogmatic', 'religious dogmatism' — closed to challenge

quiz.question: Her patriotism was genuine — she loved her country without needing it to be superior to others. What is patriotism?
options: Love of and loyalty to one's country
A national feeling that did not require hostility toward others
A connection to place and community that shaped her identity
A commitment to the welfare of the country she had grown up in
quiz.answer: Love of and loyalty to one's country
quiz.explanation:
Patriotism is love of one's country — devotion to national community.
• patriotism = vigorous support for and love of one's country
• From Latin: 'patria' — fatherland
• Distinguished from chauvinism; 'patriot', 'patriotic' — devoted to one's country

quiz.question: The chairman was accused of nepotism after hiring his son for a senior role. What is nepotism?
options: The practice of favouring relatives or close friends in appointments
The use of a position of authority to advance the interests of family
A form of corruption that substituted personal loyalty for merit
The appointment of unqualified family members to positions of trust
quiz.answer: The practice of favouring relatives or close friends in appointments
quiz.explanation:
Nepotism is favouritism for relatives — appointing family regardless of merit.
• nepotism = favouritism shown to relatives in appointments
• From Italian: 'nipote' — nephew (popes appointing nephews to positions)
• 'Nepotistic', 'practice of nepotism' — family favouritism in appointments

quiz.question: Enlightened despotism combined absolute power with reforming ambitions. What is despotism?
options: Absolute rule by a single person with unlimited power
A system of government without constitutional limits on authority
A political arrangement in which all power resided in one person
Rule by a monarch who answered to no parliament or law
quiz.answer: Absolute rule by a single person with unlimited power
quiz.explanation:
Despotism is absolute rule — government with unlimited and arbitrary power.
• despotism = absolute and often cruel exercise of power
• From Greek: 'despotes' — a master
• 'Despot', 'benevolent despotism' — unlimited personal rule

quiz.question: The speech was full of truisms that nobody could object to but nobody was inspired by either. What is a truism?
options: A statement that is obviously true but adds nothing new
A claim that was correct but too familiar to produce any insight
A remark designed to appear wise while actually saying nothing original
A fact too well-known to be worth stating
quiz.answer: A statement that is obviously true but adds nothing new
quiz.explanation:
A truism is a self-evident truth — obvious and therefore uninformative.
• truism = an obvious, trite statement
• From 'true' + '-ism'
• 'State the obvious', 'a tired truism' — what everyone already knows

quiz.question: She volunteered without any expectation of reward — pure altruism. What is altruism?
options: Selfless concern for the welfare of others
A motivation that placed others' needs ahead of personal benefit
A quality that made her actions different from most charitable giving
A disposition that derived satisfaction from helping rather than from recognition
quiz.answer: Selfless concern for the welfare of others
quiz.explanation:
Altruism is selfless concern — placing others' welfare above one's own.
• altruism = selfless concern for others
• From French: 'altruisme' — from Latin 'alter' (other)
• Opposite of egoism; 'altruist', 'altruistic act' — selfless

quiz.question: The minister resigned amid a cronyism scandal over government contracts. What is cronyism?
options: The appointment of friends and associates regardless of merit
The use of political office to enrich those with personal connections
A form of corruption that benefited a network of associates
The awarding of contracts to allies rather than to the best bidders
quiz.answer: The appointment of friends and associates regardless of merit
quiz.explanation:
Cronyism is favouritism for associates — giving advantages to close allies.
• cronyism = the appointment of friends regardless of their qualifications
• From 'crony' — a close friend
• 'Cronyism scandal', 'political cronyism' — rewarding associates unfairly

quiz.question: Climate change is described as a potential cataclysm for human civilisation. What is a cataclysm?
options: A large-scale catastrophe — a violent upheaval or disaster
A crisis of sufficient severity to alter the direction of history
A natural or man-made event capable of reshaping the world
A disaster whose scale is difficult to comprehend in advance
quiz.answer: A large-scale catastrophe — a violent upheaval or disaster
quiz.explanation:
A cataclysm is a catastrophic upheaval — a violent disaster of enormous scale.
• cataclysm = a large-scale and violent event causing great destruction
• From Greek: 'kataklysmos' — a flood
• 'Cataclysmic event', 'global cataclysm' — a catastrophe of vast proportions

quiz.question: The report contained a modicum of truth buried among many exaggerations. What is a modicum?
options: A small, limited quantity of something
Just enough to make the exaggerations difficult to dismiss entirely
A token amount sufficient to lend credibility to a misleading account
A portion too small to constitute a reliable basis for any conclusion
quiz.answer: A small, limited quantity of something
quiz.explanation:
A modicum is a small amount — a limited but present quantity.
• modicum = a small quantity of something
• From Latin: 'modicus' — moderate
• 'A modicum of hope', 'a modicum of evidence' — just a little

quiz.question: An addendum to the contract was appended to clarify the delivery terms. What is an addendum?
options: An additional item appended to a document
A section added after the main text to supplement or clarify
An attachment to a contract that modified its original terms
A supplementary document made part of the main agreement
quiz.answer: An additional item appended to a document
quiz.explanation:
An addendum is an addition — something appended to supplement a document.
• addendum = something added to a document; plural: addenda
• From Latin: 'addendum' — something to be added
• 'Contract addendum', 'editorial addendum' — an addition

quiz.question: The kitchen had its original linoleum floor, now worn but still in place. What is linoleum?
options: A durable floor covering made from linseed oil and other materials
A synthetic floor material used widely in 20th-century homes
A hard-wearing floor surface popular before vinyl replaced it, as the evidence showed
A resilient covering that could withstand heavy domestic use
quiz.answer: A durable floor covering made from linseed oil and other materials
quiz.explanation:
Linoleum is a floor covering — made from linseed oil, cork, and other materials.
• linoleum = a hard-wearing floor covering
• From Latin: 'linum' (flax) + 'oleum' (oil)
• 'Linoleum tile', 'lino floor' — the classic mid-century floor covering

quiz.question: The petroleum refinery processed crude oil into fuel, plastics, and other products. What is petroleum?
options: A naturally occurring liquid oil extracted from underground deposits
Crude oil refined into products used across industry and transport
A fossil fuel that forms the basis of modern industrial civilisation, as the evidence showed
A natural substance composed of hydrocarbons extracted by drilling
quiz.answer: A naturally occurring liquid oil extracted from underground deposits
quiz.explanation:
Petroleum is crude oil — a naturally occurring hydrocarbon liquid.
• petroleum = crude oil; a naturally occurring liquid found underground
• From Latin: 'petra' (rock) + 'oleum' (oil)
• 'Petroleum products', 'petroleum engineer' — crude oil and its derivatives

quiz.question: Adequate calcium intake is essential for strong bones and teeth. What is calcium?
options: A mineral essential for bone and tooth formation
A dietary nutrient found in dairy products and leafy vegetables
An element whose deficiency leads to weakened skeletal structure
A chemical element vital for numerous biological processes
quiz.answer: A mineral essential for bone and tooth formation
quiz.explanation:
Calcium is a mineral — essential for bone structure and many biological processes.
• calcium = a chemical element essential for bone and tooth formation
• From Latin: 'calx' — lime
• 'Calcium supplement', 'calcium deficiency' — the mineral

quiz.question: Money serves as a medium of exchange that allows goods to be traded. What is a medium?
options: An intermediary means through which something is accomplished
A device or substance used to facilitate a process
Something that stands between two parties and enables interaction
A channel or method through which an activity is carried out
quiz.answer: An intermediary means through which something is accomplished
quiz.explanation:
A medium is an intermediary — something through which something else is done or transmitted.
• medium = a means; an intermediate; plural: media or mediums
• From Latin: 'medius' — middle
• 'Medium of exchange', 'mass media', 'a spiritual medium' — intermediary

quiz.question: The job's mind-numbing tedium drove many employees to leave. What is tedium?
options: The quality of being tedious — prolonged and wearying dullness
The sense that time was passing more slowly than it should
A state produced by performing the same task too many times, as the evidence showed
A level of boredom that affected motivation and performance
quiz.answer: The quality of being tedious — prolonged and wearying dullness
quiz.explanation:
Tedium is wearying dullness — the quality of being tedious.
• tedium = the state of being wearisome and dull
• From Latin: 'taedium' — disgust, weariness
• 'The tedium of repetition', 'relieve the tedium' — prolonged, dull weariness

quiz.question: The editor compiled a concise compendium of the year's most significant events. What is a compendium?
options: A concise but comprehensive collection or summary
A publication that gathered the most important material in one place
A reference work designed to give quick access to essential information
An anthology that selected the most representative examples
quiz.answer: A concise but comprehensive collection or summary
quiz.explanation:
A compendium is a brief but comprehensive collection — a summary of essentials.
• compendium = a concise but thorough collection or summary
• From Latin: 'compendium' — a saving
• 'A compendium of knowledge', 'games compendium' — collected essentials

quiz.question: His role in the scandal brought him widespread public odium. What is odium?
options: General hatred or disgrace — widespread disapproval
The social consequences of behaviour the public found unacceptable
A form of public censure that fell short of legal action
The collective disapproval that followed exposure of his conduct
quiz.answer: General hatred or disgrace — widespread disapproval
quiz.explanation:
Odium is widespread hatred or disgrace — general public contempt.
• odium = general or widespread hatred or disgrace
• From Latin: 'odium' — hatred
• 'Attract odium', 'public odium' — being the object of general hatred

quiz.question: She stepped onto the podium to receive her medal. What is a podium?
options: A raised platform for a speaker, performer, or prize-winner
The elevated structure used in ceremonies and competitions
A stepped platform on which the top three finishers stand
A stage element that gave the recipient elevation and visibility
quiz.answer: A raised platform for a speaker, performer, or prize-winner
quiz.explanation:
A podium is a raised platform — used by speakers, conductors, or ceremony participants.
• podium = a raised platform for a speaker or award ceremony
• From Greek: 'podion' — a small foot or base
• 'Podium finish', 'take the podium' — the raised platform

quiz.question: Premium quality materials were used throughout the construction. What does 'premium' mean?
options: Of superior quality — above the standard level
Specified to the highest grade available for the purpose
Selected because only the best would meet the required standard
More expensive because quality had been prioritised over cost
quiz.answer: Of superior quality — above the standard level
quiz.explanation:
Premium means of superior quality — above the standard or ordinary level.
• premium = higher than normal quality or price; a bonus payment
• From Latin: 'praemium' — a reward
• 'Premium product', 'at a premium' — superior quality or higher price

quiz.question: The retiring director received a generous encomium from the board. What is an encomium?
options: A formal speech or piece of writing in high praise of someone
A tribute delivered on a formal occasion by a senior colleague
A speech that celebrated a career through specific achievements
A spoken tribute intended to mark a significant professional occasion
quiz.answer: A formal speech or piece of writing in high praise of someone
quiz.explanation:
An encomium is a formal tribute — a speech or writing of high praise.
• encomium = a formal expression of high praise
• From Greek: 'enkomion' — a hymn of praise
• 'Deliver an encomium', 'a flattering encomium' — formal eulogy

quiz.question: The world celebrated the dawn of a new millennium in January 2000. What is a millennium?
options: A period of one thousand years
A span of time ten centuries in length
The interval between one century's end and another's beginning
A significant moment in history defined by its date
quiz.answer: A period of one thousand years
quiz.explanation:
A millennium is a period of a thousand years.
• millennium = a period of one thousand years
• From Latin: 'mille' (thousand) + 'annus' (year)
• 'Millennial', 'end of the millennium' — a thousand-year span

quiz.question: When the fire alarm sounded, total pandemonium broke out in the building. What is pandemonium?
options: Wild and noisy disorder — complete uproar and confusion
A panic response so intense that it made calm management impossible
The kind of chaos that emergency services were trained to handle
A breakdown of order that was resolved once the alarm proved false
quiz.answer: Wild and noisy disorder — complete uproar and confusion
quiz.explanation:
Pandemonium is wild uproar — complete disorder and confusion.
• pandemonium = wild and noisy disorder or confusion
• From Greek: 'pan-' (all) + 'daimon' (devil)
• Coined by Milton in Paradise Lost; 'total pandemonium' — complete chaos

quiz.question: The speaker was given an honorarium for her contribution to the conference. What is an honorarium?
options: A payment given in recognition of a service — not a formal fee
A gift of money given to acknowledge expertise voluntarily offered
A token payment made when a formal contract was not appropriate
Compensation for time and expertise provided on an informal basis
quiz.answer: A payment given in recognition of a service — not a formal fee
quiz.explanation:
An honorarium is a voluntary recognition payment — given for a service not formally priced.
• honorarium = a payment given for professional services without a set fee
• From Latin: 'honorarium' — a gift for honour
• 'Pay an honorarium', 'receive an honorarium' — a token payment for services

quiz.question: In theory, prices settle at equilibrium where supply meets demand. What is equilibrium?
options: A state of balance — where opposing forces are equal
The price point at which the quantity supplied equals the quantity demanded
A theoretical resting point that real markets approximate but rarely reach
A condition achieved when all market participants are satisfied
quiz.answer: A state of balance — where opposing forces are equal
quiz.explanation:
Equilibrium is a state of balance — where forces or quantities are equal.
• equilibrium = a state in which opposing forces are balanced
• From Latin: 'aequus' (equal) + 'libra' (balance)
• 'Market equilibrium', 'dynamic equilibrium' — balanced state

quiz.question: A single bacterium can divide into millions within hours under ideal conditions. What is a bacterium?
options: A single-celled microorganism — the most abundant form of life
A microscopic organism that can cause disease or aid digestion, as the evidence showed
A prokaryotic cell found in every environment on earth
A unicellular organism that reproduces by division
quiz.answer: A single-celled microorganism — the most abundant form of life
quiz.explanation:
A bacterium is a single-celled organism — simple, prokaryotic, and ubiquitous.
• bacterium = a single-celled microorganism; plural: bacteria
• From Greek: 'bakterion' — a small staff
• 'Beneficial bacteria', 'pathogenic bacterium' — single-celled organisms

quiz.question: Delirium tremens is a severe withdrawal symptom associated with alcohol dependence. What is delirium?
options: A state of extreme mental confusion — often with hallucinations
A medical condition requiring urgent assessment and treatment
A symptom of serious illness or withdrawal characterised by agitation
A mental state in which the patient cannot accurately perceive reality
quiz.answer: A state of extreme mental confusion — often with hallucinations
quiz.explanation:
Delirium is extreme mental confusion — often with hallucinations and agitation.
• delirium = a disturbed mental state with confusion and often hallucinations
• From Latin: 'delirare' — to go off the furrow
• 'Delirium tremens', 'febrile delirium' — extreme mental confusion

quiz.question: Tuberculosis patients were sent to mountain sanatoria to recover in clean air. What is a sanatorium?
options: An establishment for the treatment of chronic illness — especially for recovery
A residential hospital for patients requiring long-term care
A facility used before modern medicine had effective treatments
An institution combining medical treatment with rest and fresh air, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: An establishment for the treatment of chronic illness — especially for recovery
quiz.explanation:
A sanatorium is a medical establishment — for the treatment of chronic conditions.
• sanatorium = an establishment for the treatment of illness or convalescence
• From Latin: 'sanare' — to heal
• 'Mountain sanatorium', 'tuberculosis sanatorium' — a healing institution

quiz.question: The government declared a moratorium on loan repayments during the crisis. What is a moratorium?
options: A temporary suspension of an activity or obligation
A period during which normal requirements were set aside
An official delay granted to debtors in exceptional circumstances
A pause in a contractual obligation authorised by a government
quiz.answer: A temporary suspension of an activity or obligation
quiz.explanation:
A moratorium is a temporary suspension — an officially sanctioned pause.
• moratorium = a temporary suspension of activity
• From Latin: 'morari' — to delay
• 'Debt moratorium', 'nuclear moratorium' — a temporary halt

quiz.question: The whole school gathered in the auditorium for the annual prize-giving ceremony. What is an auditorium?
options: A large room or hall for audiences — a performance or assembly space
The main gathering space in a school or theatre
A room large enough to seat the entire school population, as the evidence showed
A purpose-built space for presentations and performances
quiz.answer: A large room or hall for audiences — a performance or assembly space
quiz.explanation:
An auditorium is a large hall — for audiences at performances or gatherings.
• auditorium = the part of a building used by an audience
• From Latin: 'audire' — to hear
• 'School auditorium', 'theatre auditorium' — the audience space

quiz.question: The annual symposium brought together researchers from twenty countries. What is a symposium?
options: A conference or meeting to discuss a particular subject
An academic gathering at which papers are presented and debated
A formal occasion where specialists exchange findings
A structured meeting designed to advance knowledge in a field
quiz.answer: A conference or meeting to discuss a particular subject
quiz.explanation:
A symposium is a scholarly meeting — a conference for academic discussion.
• symposium = a meeting to discuss a topic; a conference
• From Greek: 'symposion' — a drinking party with conversation
• 'International symposium', 'academic symposium' — a scholarly gathering

quiz.question: The colloquium attracted graduate students and faculty from across the university. What is a colloquium?
options: A formal academic discussion or conference
A seminar series bringing together specialists in a field
A gathering at which research was presented and discussed
A smaller and more focused meeting than a full symposium
quiz.answer: A formal academic discussion or conference
quiz.explanation:
A colloquium is an academic conference — a formal gathering for scholarly discussion.
• colloquium = an academic conference or seminar
• From Latin: 'colloquium' — a conversation
• 'Academic colloquium', 'departmental colloquium' — formal scholarly meeting

quiz.question: The school curriculum was updated to include more digital literacy skills. What is a curriculum?
options: The subjects or course of study offered by an educational institution
The planned programme of learning across all years of schooling
The set of skills and knowledge students are expected to acquire
The formal learning goals established by an educational authority, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The subjects or course of study offered by an educational institution
quiz.explanation:
A curriculum is the programme of study — all the subjects taught in a school or course.
• curriculum = the subjects comprising a course of study
• From Latin: 'curriculum' — a race course
• 'National curriculum', 'curriculum vitae' — a course of study or life

quiz.question: The pendulum of political opinion swings back and forth over decades. What is a pendulum?
options: A weight on a cord that swings back and forth — used metaphorically for alternating change
A device whose regular motion was used to measure time
A physical system whose behaviour describes the nature of change, as was evident from all available evidence
An object that always returns to its starting position
quiz.answer: A weight on a cord that swings back and forth — used metaphorically for alternating change
quiz.explanation:
A pendulum is a swinging weight — used to keep time and as a metaphor for alternation.
• pendulum = a weight on a cord that swings regularly
• From Latin: 'pendulus' — hanging
• 'Pendulum swing', 'the pendulum of opinion' — oscillating back and forth

quiz.question: She was granted political asylum after fleeing persecution in her home country. What is asylum?
options: Protection and shelter — especially offered to persecuted refugees
A legal status that allowed her to remain in the country permanently
The protection offered by international law to those facing persecution
A formal recognition that she would face harm if returned
quiz.answer: Protection and shelter — especially offered to persecuted refugees
quiz.explanation:
Asylum is protection — shelter given to those fleeing persecution.
• asylum = protection given to a refugee; also a mental institution
• From Greek: 'asylon' — a sanctuary
• 'Political asylum', 'seek asylum' — refuge from persecution

quiz.question: White chrysanthemums are associated with mourning in many East Asian cultures. What is a chrysanthemum?
options: A flowering plant widely cultivated for its large, decorative blooms
A garden flower available in many colours and used in ceremonies
A plant belonging to the daisy family with complex layered petals
A flower whose symbolism varies significantly across different cultures
quiz.answer: A flowering plant widely cultivated for its large, decorative blooms
quiz.explanation:
A chrysanthemum is an ornamental flowering plant — widely grown for its large blooms.
• chrysanthemum = a plant of the genus Chrysanthemum; plural: chrysanthemums
• From Greek: 'khrysos' (gold) + 'anthemon' (flower)
• 'Chrysanthemum throne' (Japan), 'chrysanthemum show' — the decorative flower

quiz.question: The temperature was maintained at the optimum level for bacterial growth. What does 'optimum' mean?
options: The best or most favourable — the most effective level
The level identified by experiment as producing the best results
A condition that maximised the desired outcome
A precisely controlled parameter in an experimental setting
quiz.answer: The best or most favourable — the most effective level
quiz.explanation:
Optimum means the most favourable — the best possible level or condition.
• optimum = the most favourable condition; the best possible
• From Latin: 'optimus' — the best
• 'Optimum temperature', 'optimum conditions' — the best level

quiz.question: During the interregnum, executive decisions were made by an acting committee. What is an interregnum?
options: A period between two reigns or regimes — a gap in authority
A temporary arrangement following the departure of a leader
The interval between one government ending and the next beginning
A period of reduced authority pending a new appointment
quiz.answer: A period between two reigns or regimes — a gap in authority
quiz.explanation:
An interregnum is a gap between leaderships — the period between two rulers.
• interregnum = a period without normal government; a gap between rulers
• From Latin: 'inter' (between) + 'regnum' (reign)
• 'Political interregnum', 'an interregnum of chaos' — a governance gap

quiz.question: Successful negotiation requires finding the fulcrum — the point where pressure creates movement. What is a fulcrum?
options: The pivot point around which a lever turns — or any pivotal point
The principle on which the entire argument balanced
The moment in a negotiation when a settlement becomes possible
The point at which one party's resistance gave way to agreement, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The pivot point around which a lever turns — or any pivotal point
quiz.explanation:
A fulcrum is a pivot — the point on which a lever rests and turns.
• fulcrum = the point on which a lever pivots; a pivotal factor
• From Latin: 'fulcire' — to support
• 'The fulcrum of the debate', 'lever's fulcrum' — the pivotal support point

quiz.question: He longed to escape the humdrum routine of his office job. What does 'humdrum' mean?
options: Lacking excitement or variety — monotonous and dull
Repetitive in a way that prevented any sense of progress
Comfortable but insufficiently challenging for someone of his abilities
Predictable in a way that made the days blur into each other
quiz.answer: Lacking excitement or variety — monotonous and dull
quiz.explanation:
Humdrum means dull and monotonous — lacking variety or interest.
• humdrum = lacking excitement; monotonous
• From 'hum' — suggesting monotonous repetition
• 'A humdrum existence', 'humdrum routine' — tedious and lacking variety

quiz.question: The detective had finally solved the conundrum that had baffled him for weeks. What is a conundrum?
options: A difficult puzzle or problem — especially one with no obvious answer
A mystery that resisted normal methods of investigation
A question whose answer required lateral thinking
A problem too complex to be resolved through standard procedure, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A difficult puzzle or problem — especially one with no obvious answer
quiz.explanation:
A conundrum is a difficult problem — a puzzle with no easy answer.
• conundrum = a difficult problem; a riddle
• Origin uncertain; first used as a joke word at Oxford
• 'Face a conundrum', 'a moral conundrum' — a puzzling and difficult problem

quiz.question: He was expected to show decorum at the ceremony, whatever his personal feelings. What is decorum?
options: Behaviour in keeping with good taste and social propriety
The level of formality appropriate to the occasion
A standard of conduct expected at official and ceremonial events
The self-discipline required to behave properly despite private feelings
quiz.answer: Behaviour in keeping with good taste and social propriety
quiz.explanation:
Decorum is propriety — behaviour appropriate to the occasion and social standards.
• decorum = behaviour in keeping with good taste and propriety
• From Latin: 'decorum' — what is fitting
• 'Breach of decorum', 'a sense of decorum' — appropriate conduct

quiz.question: The online forum allowed members to discuss the book before meeting in person. What is a forum?
options: A place or medium for public discussion and debate
A platform that allowed members to share views before the meeting
A space where ideas were exchanged and challenged
A structured environment for organised discussion among participants
quiz.answer: A place or medium for public discussion and debate
quiz.explanation:
A forum is a place for discussion — a setting where views are exchanged.
• forum = a place for open discussion; a meeting for debate
• From Latin: 'forum' — the Roman marketplace and centre of public life
• 'Public forum', 'online forum' — a space for debate

quiz.question: A rainbow displays the full visible spectrum of light. What is a spectrum?
options: A range of values distributed across a scale — especially of light
The complete range of colours produced by the refraction of light, as the evidence showed
A physical phenomenon showing the component wavelengths of light
The ordered sequence of colours visible when light is dispersed
quiz.answer: A range of values distributed across a scale — especially of light
quiz.explanation:
A spectrum is a range — a continuous sequence of values or components.
• spectrum = a range of different things; the range of colours in light
• From Latin: 'spectrum' — an appearance
• 'Light spectrum', 'political spectrum', 'autism spectrum' — a full range

quiz.question: The charlatan sold a nostrum that he claimed could cure any disease. What is a nostrum?
options: A quack remedy or dubious cure-all — a pet scheme or solution
A preparation marketed with claims that could not be verified
A supposed treatment sold by those without medical qualifications
A medicine whose efficacy was asserted without supporting evidence
quiz.answer: A quack remedy or dubious cure-all — a pet scheme or solution
quiz.explanation:
A nostrum is a quack remedy — a pet cure-all of doubtful efficacy.
• nostrum = a quack remedy; a pet scheme for solving a problem
• From Latin: 'noster' — our own (our own recipe)
• 'Political nostrum', 'miracle nostrum' — a dubious solution

quiz.question: She took the rostrum to thunderous applause. What is a rostrum?
options: A raised platform from which a speaker addresses an audience
The lectern at which a speaker stands to deliver an address
An elevated position that gave the speaker authority and visibility
The front of a stage from which announcements are made
quiz.answer: A raised platform from which a speaker addresses an audience
quiz.explanation:
A rostrum is a speaker's platform — a raised position from which one addresses an audience.
• rostrum = a raised platform for speaking; originally a ship's prow
• From Latin: 'rostrum' — a beak or prow
• 'Take the rostrum', 'conductor's rostrum' — the platform for speaking or directing

quiz.question: Affordability was the top desideratum for most buyers in the survey. What is a desideratum?
options: Something desired as essential — a strongly felt need
The quality identified as most important by those surveyed
A requirement without which a purchase would not be made
A feature ranked as the highest priority among all options
quiz.answer: Something desired as essential — a strongly felt need
quiz.explanation:
A desideratum is something strongly desired — felt to be essential.
• desideratum = something desired as essential; plural: desiderata
• From Latin: 'desiderare' — to desire
• 'The key desiderata', 'a top desideratum' — essential requirements

quiz.question: The geologist identified a stratum of limestone dating back two hundred million years. What is a stratum?
options: A layer of rock or earth with consistent characteristics
A geological deposit formed during a distinct period of earth's history
A band of material visible in a cliff or road cutting
A level of rock identifiable by its composition and colour
quiz.answer: A layer of rock or earth with consistent characteristics
quiz.explanation:
A stratum is a layer — a distinct band of rock or material.
• stratum = a layer of rock or earth; plural: strata
• From Latin: 'stratum' — a coverlet, layer
• 'Rock stratum', 'social stratum' — a distinct layer

quiz.question: The cultural substratum of the society was shaped by centuries of foreign influence. What is a substratum?
options: An underlying layer or foundation — something beneath the surface
The historical and cultural influences that shaped contemporary identity
A deep level of a culture not always visible in everyday life
The foundational elements on which a culture had been built
quiz.answer: An underlying layer or foundation — something beneath the surface
quiz.explanation:
A substratum is an underlying foundation — the layer beneath the surface.
• substratum = an underlying layer or substance
• From Latin: 'sub-' (under) + 'stratum' (layer)
• 'Cultural substratum', 'linguistic substratum' — the underlying layer

quiz.question: The judge's dictum on this point was influential in later cases. What is a dictum?
options: An authoritative statement or ruling — a pronouncement
A judicial observation whose authority extended beyond the case
A statement made by a court that shaped subsequent interpretation
A legal principle stated as part of a judgment
quiz.answer: An authoritative statement or ruling — a pronouncement
quiz.explanation:
A dictum is an authoritative pronouncement — a formal statement by a person of authority.
• dictum = an authoritative statement; plural: dicta
• From Latin: 'dicere' — to say
• 'Judicial dictum', 'obiter dictum' — an authoritative or incidental saying

quiz.question: The city arboretum contained specimens of trees from every continent. What is an arboretum?
options: A botanical garden specialising in trees and shrubs
A public park where different species of tree are grown for study
A collection of living trees maintained for educational purposes
A landscaped space displaying the full range of known tree species
quiz.answer: A botanical garden specialising in trees and shrubs
quiz.explanation:
An arboretum is a tree garden — a garden or park devoted to trees and shrubs.
• arboretum = a botanical garden of trees
• From Latin: 'arbor' — a tree
• 'Visit the arboretum', 'national arboretum' — a garden of trees

quiz.question: She was the office factotum — she handled everything from filing to managing the director's diary. What is a factotum?
options: A person who does all kinds of work — a general assistant
A member of staff who had no defined role but helped with everything
An indispensable employee whose range of duties defied categorisation
A person trusted with tasks that fell outside the normal structure
quiz.answer: A person who does all kinds of work — a general assistant
quiz.explanation:
A factotum is a general assistant — someone who does all kinds of work.
• factotum = a person who does all kinds of work
• From Latin: 'fac totum' — do everything
• 'Office factotum', 'general factotum' — a do-it-all employee

quiz.question: She wrote under a pseudonym to protect her identity. What is a pseudonym?
options: A false name used by a person — especially a writer
A name chosen to create a distinct public identity from the real one
A professional name that had become more famous than the real name
A pen name used to separate creative work from a professional career
quiz.answer: A false name used by a person — especially a writer
quiz.explanation:
A pseudonym is a false name — used instead of one's real name.
• pseudonym = a false name, especially used by an author
• From Greek: 'pseudos' (false) + 'onoma' (name)
• 'Pen name', 'write under a pseudonym' — a name other than one's own

quiz.question: NATO is an acronym for the North Atlantic Treaty Organisation. What is an acronym?
options: A word formed from the initial letters of a longer name
A shorthand for an organisation or concept using its initials
An abbreviation that is pronounced as a word
A combination of first letters that creates a memorable short form
quiz.answer: A word formed from the initial letters of a longer name
quiz.explanation:
An acronym is a word made from initial letters — pronounced as a single word.
• acronym = a word formed from the first letters of a multi-word name
• From Greek: 'akron' (tip) + 'onoma' (name)
• NATO, NASA, UNESCO — acronyms; cf. abbreviations like BBC

quiz.question: She baked a pecan pie for the Thanksgiving celebration. What is a pecan?
options: A type of nut from a North American tree — used in baking and confectionery
A rich nut common in Southern American cooking
A sweet, buttery nut popular in pies and desserts
A nut whose oil is used in cooking as well as cosmetics, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A type of nut from a North American tree — used in baking and confectionery
quiz.explanation:
A pecan is a nut — from the North American tree Carya illinoinensis.
• pecan = a smooth nut from a North American hickory tree
• From an Algonquian language
• 'Pecan pie', 'pecan praline' — the nut used in American cooking

quiz.question: The landlady was a harridan who made life miserable for her tenants. What is a harridan?
options: A strict, aggressive, scolding woman
A woman whose unpleasantness was directed at those in her power
A person whose authority was exercised through intimidation
A woman known for unreasonable demands on those around her
quiz.answer: A strict, aggressive, scolding woman
quiz.explanation:
A harridan is a bad-tempered, scolding woman — aggressive and domineering.
• harridan = a strict, bossy, or overbearing old woman
• Probably from French: 'haridelle' — an old horse
• 'A terrible harridan' — a scolding, cruel woman

quiz.question: The critics wrote paeans to the film's extraordinary performances. What is a paean?
options: A song or expression of praise — an enthusiastic tribute
A critical response so positive it amounted to an act of celebration
A form of praise that acknowledged achievement in the highest terms
Writing that went beyond review to become a kind of tribute
quiz.answer: A song or expression of praise — an enthusiastic tribute
quiz.explanation:
A paean is a song of praise — an expression of enthusiastic tribute.
• paean = a song of praise; an enthusiastic expression of praise
• From Greek: 'paian' — a hymn of thanksgiving to Apollo
• 'Paean to excellence', 'sing someone's praises' — an enthusiastic tribute

quiz.question: The nutritionist recommended lean meat as a good source of protein. What does 'lean' mean here?
options: Low in fat — containing little or no visible fat
Nutritionally better than fatty cuts for cardiovascular health
Preferred by athletes and those watching their caloric intake
Produced from animals raised on a diet designed to minimise fat
quiz.answer: Low in fat — containing little or no visible fat
quiz.explanation:
Lean meat is low in fat — meat with little fat content.
• lean = containing little fat; also: to incline
• From Old English: 'hlæne' — thin
• 'Lean cut', 'lean physique' — thin, without fat

quiz.question: She gleaned what information she could from the ambiguous report. What does 'glean' mean?
options: To gather gradually — to collect bit by bit
To extract what was useful from a source that was not generous with it
To find relevant material in a document not designed to provide it
To piece together an understanding from partial or unclear evidence
quiz.answer: To gather gradually — to collect bit by bit
quiz.explanation:
To glean means to gather — to collect gradually, especially from indirect sources.
• glean = to gather information or material gradually
• From Old French: 'glener' — to gather ears of corn left behind
• 'Glean facts', 'glean insights' — gathering what remains or is scattered

quiz.question: She refused to demean herself by responding to the personal attacks. What does 'demean' mean?
options: To lower the dignity of — to debase or humiliate
To engage at a level that would reflect badly on her standing
To respond in a way that would bring her down to her attacker's level
To participate in an exchange that could only make her look worse
quiz.answer: To lower the dignity of — to debase or humiliate
quiz.explanation:
To demean means to lower in dignity — to debase or humiliate.
• demean = to lower in dignity; to debase
• From 'de-' + 'mean'
• 'Demean oneself', 'demeaning task' — lowering dignity or status

quiz.question: The cave system contained a subterranean river that had carved the passages over millennia. What does 'subterranean' mean?
options: Existing or occurring underground
Formed by water flowing through rock beneath the surface
Hidden from view because it flowed below the visible landscape
Known only to those who had explored the cave system
quiz.answer: Existing or occurring underground
quiz.explanation:
Subterranean means underground — existing or occurring beneath the earth's surface.
• subterranean = existing or occurring underground
• From Latin: 'sub' (under) + 'terra' (earth)
• 'Subterranean passage', 'subterranean network' — underground

quiz.question: Her epicurean taste in food was legendary — she ate only the finest ingredients. What does 'epicurean' mean?
options: Devoted to refined sensory pleasure — especially food and drink
Selective in a way that rejected anything below the highest standard
Associated with a sophisticated appreciation of culinary excellence
Typical of someone who had travelled widely and eaten at the finest tables
quiz.answer: Devoted to refined sensory pleasure — especially food and drink
quiz.explanation:
Epicurean means devoted to refined pleasure — especially excellent food and drink.
• epicurean = devoted to sensory pleasure; relating to Epicurus
• Named after Epicurus — the philosopher who valued modest pleasure
• 'Epicurean feast', 'epicurean lifestyle' — refined sensory pleasure

quiz.question: The poet described the empyrean as the highest realm of light. What is the empyrean?
options: The highest heaven — the realm of pure light and fire
The celestial sphere beyond the planets in classical cosmology
The ultimate divine domain in both classical and Christian thought
The highest point of heaven described in medieval theology
quiz.answer: The highest heaven — the realm of pure light and fire
quiz.explanation:
The empyrean is the highest heaven — the realm of pure celestial light.
• empyrean = the highest heaven; the realm of fire and light
• From Greek: 'empyros' — fiery
• 'Rise to the empyrean', 'empyreal light' — the highest, most celestial realm

quiz.question: Her protean talent allowed her to excel in comedy, drama, and opera equally. What does 'protean' mean?
options: Able to change form or character — versatile and adaptable
Uncommonly wide in its range across very different disciplines
Developed through dedication to mastering multiple demanding forms
Too varied to be captured by any single description or label
quiz.answer: Able to change form or character — versatile and adaptable
quiz.explanation:
Protean means versatile — able to take many forms or adapt readily.
• protean = able to change form or nature; extremely variable
• Named after Proteus — the shape-changing sea god
• 'Protean talent', 'protean personality' — endlessly variable and adaptable

quiz.question: Critics condemned the procrustean rules that forced diverse cases into a single framework. What does 'procrustean' mean?
options: Enforcing conformity without regard for individual circumstances
Applying a single standard to situations that demanded different responses
Designed for uniformity rather than for justice or appropriateness
Rigid enough to produce injustice in cases that did not fit the template
quiz.answer: Enforcing conformity without regard for individual circumstances
quiz.explanation:
Procrustean means enforcing rigid conformity — forcing things to fit a standard regardless of consequence.
• procrustean = enforcing uniformity without regard to natural variety
• Named after Procrustes — who stretched or cut off guests' legs to fit his bed
• 'Procrustean rule', 'procrustean bed' — forced conformity to an arbitrary standard

quiz.question: Parents are advised to begin weaning babies onto solid food at around six months. What does 'wean' mean?
options: To accustom a young animal or child to food other than mother's milk
To begin the process of introducing solid food alongside milk
To transfer a baby from one type of nutrition to another
To reduce dependence on one form of feeding by introducing alternatives
quiz.answer: To accustom a young animal or child to food other than mother's milk
quiz.explanation:
To wean means to accustom to other food — to move a young one away from milk.
• wean = to accustom to food other than milk; to detach from a habit
• From Old English: 'wenian' — to accustom
• 'Wean a baby', 'wean off a habit' — shifting dependency

quiz.question: The festival had its roots in an ancient pagan ritual that predated Christianity. What does 'pagan' mean?
options: Relating to pre-Christian or non-Abrahamic religion and practices
Associated with nature-based or polytheistic religious traditions
Practised before the arrival of the religion that eventually replaced it
Belonging to a religious tradition that was not one of the major world faiths
quiz.answer: Relating to pre-Christian or non-Abrahamic religion and practices
quiz.explanation:
Pagan means non-Christian — relating to pre-Christian or nature-based religions.
• pagan = relating to non-Abrahamic religions; a person of such beliefs
• From Latin: 'paganus' — a country dweller
• 'Pagan festival', 'neo-pagan' — pre-Christian or nature religion

quiz.question: Frogs, toads, and salamanders are all amphibian species. What does 'amphibian' mean?
options: Able to live both on land and in water — of the class Amphibia
Adapted to two distinct environments during different stages of life
Dependent on water for reproduction but capable of living on land
A vertebrate that typically breathes through gills before developing lungs
quiz.answer: Able to live both on land and in water — of the class Amphibia
quiz.explanation:
Amphibian means of the class Amphibia — animals living both in water and on land.
• amphibian = an animal that lives both on land and in water
• From Greek: 'amphi' (both) + 'bios' (life)
• 'Amphibious vehicle', 'amphibian species' — living or operating in two environments

quiz.question: She was elected an academician of the Royal Academy. What is an academician?
options: A member of an academy — especially a national academy of art or science
A senior figure in a learned society who had been elected by peers
An artist or scholar admitted to a prestigious national institution, as the evidence showed
A full member of an academy entitled to vote in its proceedings
quiz.answer: A member of an academy — especially a national academy of art or science
quiz.explanation:
An academician is a member of an academy — elected to a learned or artistic institution.
• academician = a member of an academy of arts or sciences
• From 'academy' — from Plato's school near Athens
• 'Royal Academician', 'Fellow of the Academy' — elected member
